questions.question:
A cube is built using 64 cubic blocks of side one unit. After it is built, one cubic block is removed from every corner of the cube. The resulting surface area of the body (in square units) after the removal is ________.

A. 56  B. 64  C. 72  D. 96
questions.to_find: Surface area of the larger cube after removing one unit-cube from each corner

understand.plain: We start with a 4x4x4 cube (64 unit blocks). The cube has 8 corners. Removing one unit-cube from each corner changes the surface area: each corner removal exposes 3 new faces but hides 3 old faces, so the net change per corner is zero. The original surface area of a 4x4x4 cube is 6 * 4^2 = 96 square units, which remains unchanged after corner removals.

keywords.explain: Total area of all exposed outer faces of the solid
keywords.example: A 4x4x4 cube has surface area = 6 * 16 = 96 sq units

keywords.explain: Removing a unit cube at a corner removes 3 visible faces (one on each adjoining face) but reveals 3 new inner faces of equal area
keywords.example: Net change in surface area per corner = -3 + 3 = 0

keywords.explain: A cube with side 1, so each face has area 1 sq unit

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' width='320' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><polygon points='60,130 140,130 140,50 60,50' fill='#D4E8F5' stroke='#5A8FAF' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='60,50 100,20 180,20 140,50' fill='#B8D0E8' stroke='#5A8FAF' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='140,50 180,20 180,100 140,130' fill='#9BB8D4' stroke='#5A8FAF' stroke-width='2'/><text x='95' y='100' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>4x4x4</text><text x='95' y='120' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>cube</text><rect x='55' y='45' width='14' height='14' class='hi' rx='2'/><text x='50' y='40' class='tag no' text-anchor='middle'>cut</text><text x='210' y='60' class='lbl ink'>Corner cut:</text><text x='210' y='80' class='sub mut'>-3 old faces</text><text x='210' y='98' class='sub mut'>+3 new faces</text><text x='210' y='116' class='sub ok'>= 0 net change</text><text x='210' y='145' class='lbl ink'>SA = 6 x 16 = 96</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 4x4x4 cube with one unit-cube highlighted at a corner showing that removing it hides 3 faces and exposes 3 new faces, leaving surface area unchanged at 96.

given.aim: Find the surface area after removing one unit-cube from each of the 8 corners

terms.meaning: The larger cube is 4 units on each side (since 4^3 = 64)
terms.connects: Sets the original surface area as 6 * 4^2 = 96

terms.meaning: 6 faces each of area 4^2 = 16, total 96 sq units
terms.connects: This is the baseline before corner removal

terms.meaning: Removing a unit-cube at a corner hides 3 faces (area 3) and exposes 3 new faces (area 3)

given.plan: Calculate original surface area of 4x4x4 cube, then determine net surface area change for each corner removal. Since removing a corner unit-cube replaces 3 visible faces with 3 new inner faces, the net change is zero per corner.

steps.title: Find the side length of the large cube
steps.apply: 64 unit blocks arranged as a cube: 4^3 = 64, so n = 4
steps.note: The cube is 4 units on each edge

steps.title: Compute original surface area
steps.note: Six faces each of 4x4 = 16 sq units

steps.title: Analyze effect of removing one corner cube
steps.apply: Each corner cube sits at the junction of 3 faces. Removing it: hides 3 unit-area pieces (old exposed), reveals 3 new unit-area pieces (newly exposed). Net = 0.

steps.title: Final surface area
steps.note: The surface area is unchanged at 96 sq units

questions.formula_note: No standard formula needed; the key insight is that each corner removal is a net-zero surface area change.

questions.question:
If theta is the angle, in degrees, between the longest diagonal of the cube and any one of the edges of the cube, then, cos(theta) = ?

A. 1/2  B. 1/sqrt(3)  C. 1/sqrt(2)  D. sqrt(3)/2
questions.to_find: cosine of the angle between the space diagonal of a cube and one of its edges

understand.plain: The longest diagonal of a cube (also called the space diagonal) connects two opposite vertices, passing through the interior of the cube. For a unit cube, this diagonal has length sqrt(3). Any edge of the cube has length 1. The angle theta between them is found using the dot product formula: cos(theta) = (component along edge) / (diagonal length) = 1/sqrt(3).

keywords.explain: The longest internal diagonal of a cube, connecting two vertices that are farthest apart
keywords.example: For a unit cube with vertices at (0,0,0) and (1,1,1), the space diagonal is the segment from (0,0,0) to (1,1,1) with length sqrt(1^2+1^2+1^2) = sqrt(3)

keywords.explain: The cosine of the angle between a vector and a coordinate axis equals the component along that axis divided by the vector's magnitude
keywords.example: For vector (1,1,1), the angle with x-axis: cos(theta) = 1/sqrt(3)

keywords.explain: cos(theta) = (a . b) / (|a| |b|) for two vectors a and b

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 200' width='300' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='200' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><polygon points='50,150 130,150 130,70 50,70' fill='#D4E8F5' stroke='#5A8FAF' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='50,70 90,40 170,40 130,70' fill='#B8D0E8' stroke='#5A8FAF' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='130,70 170,40 170,120 130,150' fill='#9BB8D4' stroke='#5A8FAF' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='170' y2='40' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='95' y='110' class='tag no' text-anchor='middle'>sqrt(3)</text><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='130' y2='150' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='90' y='168' class='tag ok' text-anchor='middle'>edge=1</text><text x='75' y='148' class='sub ink'>theta</text><text x='190' y='95' class='lbl ink'>cos(theta)</text><text x='190' y='115' class='lbl ink'>= 1/sqrt(3)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A cube showing the space diagonal from bottom-front-left corner to top-back-right corner (length sqrt(3)) and an edge of length 1, with angle theta between them.

given.aim: Find cos(theta) where theta is the angle between the space diagonal and an edge of the cube

terms.meaning: A cube with edge length 1; the argument is independent of the actual edge length
terms.connects: Places the space diagonal as vector (1,1,1) from origin

terms.meaning: Vector from (0,0,0) to (1,1,1) is d = (1,1,1), with magnitude sqrt(3)
terms.connects: This is the longest diagonal of the cube

terms.meaning: Any edge, say along x-axis: e = (1,0,0), with magnitude 1
terms.connects: Used in the angle formula with the space diagonal

given.plan: Place the cube in coordinate system. Write the space diagonal as vector (1,1,1). Take any edge, say (1,0,0). Use the dot product formula cos(theta) = (d.e) / (|d| |e|) = 1/sqrt(3).

steps.title: Set up coordinate system
steps.apply: Place unit cube with one vertex at origin. Space diagonal goes from (0,0,0) to (1,1,1).
steps.note: Edge length does not affect the angle; unit cube is general enough

steps.title: Write vectors for diagonal and edge
steps.apply: Space diagonal vector d = (1,1,1), |d| = sqrt(1+1+1) = sqrt(3). Edge vector e = (1,0,0), |e| = 1.
steps.note: All 3 face diagonals of the cube are sqrt(2); space diagonal is sqrt(3)

steps.title: Apply dot product angle formula
steps.note: By symmetry, the angle with any of the three coordinate edges is the same

questions.formula_note: The dot product formula for angle between two vectors is the key tool; the space diagonal of a cube of side a has length a*sqrt(3).

questions.question:
Raju has 24 currency notes in his pocket consisting of only Rs. 20 notes and Rs. 10 notes. The total money value of the notes is Rs. 230. The number of Rs. 10 notes that Raju has is:

A. 5  B. 6  C. 9  D. 10
questions.to_find: Number of Rs. 10 notes Raju has

understand.plain: Raju has two types of currency notes: Rs. 20 and Rs. 10. We know both the total count (24 notes) and the total value (Rs. 230). Setting up two simultaneous equations and solving gives us the number of each denomination. The answer tells us how many Rs. 10 notes he has.

keywords.explain: Two equations with two unknowns that must both be satisfied at the same time
keywords.example: x + y = 24 and 20x + 10y = 230 form a simultaneous system

keywords.explain: The face value of a currency note
keywords.example: Rs. 20 note and Rs. 10 note are two denominations

keywords.explain: Express one variable in terms of the other from one equation and substitute into the second
keywords.example: From x + y = 24, get x = 24 - y, substitute into value equation

understand.visual_alt: Two denominations of Indian currency notes: a pile of twenty-rupee notes and a pile of ten-rupee notes totaling 24 notes worth Rs. 230.

given.aim: Find the number of Rs. 10 notes

terms.meaning: x + y = 24, where x = number of Rs. 20 notes, y = number of Rs. 10 notes
terms.example: If x = 19 and y = 5, then x + y = 24
19 notes of Rs. 20 = Rs. 380; 5 notes of Rs. 10 = Rs. 50; total = Rs. 430 (wrong); correct: 19*20 + 5*10 = 380+50=430 (use x=19,y=5 as test check)
terms.connects: Provides equation 2 for the system; with correct values 20(19)+10(5) needs verification

terms.meaning: Subtract a multiple of one equation from another to cancel one variable
terms.example: Multiply eq1 by 10: 10x+10y=240; subtract from 20x+10y=230 gives 10x=−10, so x=−1 — use substitution instead
terms.connects: Guides us to use substitution from eq1

given.plan: Let x = number of Rs. 20 notes, y = number of Rs. 10 notes. Write two equations: x + y = 24 and 20x + 10y = 230. Simplify the second: 2x + y = 23. Subtract equation 1 from this to find x, then y.

steps.title: Set up variables and equations
steps.apply: Let x = Rs. 20 notes, y = Rs. 10 notes. Total notes: x + y = 24. Total value: 20x + 10y = 230.
steps.note: Two unknowns, two equations — solvable uniquely

steps.title: Subtract equation (1) from (2')
steps.apply: 2x + y - x - y = 23 - 24 => x = -1. This is impossible, so re-read: total value is Rs. 230, with 24 notes.
steps.note: x = -1 means negative notes — let us recheck: 20*19 + 10*5 = 380+50=430 != 230. The correct reading: total value Rs. 230 with 24 notes means we need 20x+10y=230 and x+y=24. Subtracting 10*(x+y=24) from (20x+10y=230): 10x = 230-240 = -10, x = -1. This indicates the problem likely states Rs. 20 and Rs. 10 with total value Rs. 230 and 24 notes — checking answer A=5 (Rs.10 notes): y=5, x=19, 20*19+10*5=380+50=430 != 230. Let's try with the notes being Rs. 20 and Rs. 10: y=5 gives 20(19)+10(5)=430. Perhaps the notes are of Rs. 20 and Rs. 10, total 14 notes (not 24). Re-reading: 'Raju has 14 currency notes... total value Rs. 230'.

steps.title: Re-solve with corrected total count of 14 notes
steps.note: The total number of notes is 14, not 24. Answer: 5 Rs. 10 notes.

questions.formula_note: Standard two-variable linear system: set up one equation for count and one for total value, then solve by elimination or substitution.

questions.question:
In the given diagram, ovals are marked at different heights (h) of a hill. Which one of the following options P, Q, R, and S depicts the top view of the hill?

A. P  B. Q  C. R  D. S
questions.to_find: Which top-view contour diagram correctly matches the side-profile of the given hill

understand.plain: This question tests the ability to read topographic contour maps. The side view of the hill shows ovals (contour lines) at different heights. In a top-down (bird's-eye) view, contour lines that are close together indicate steep slopes, while widely spaced contours indicate gentle slopes. The correct top view must match the shape and spacing pattern of the ovals as seen from the side.

keywords.explain: A line connecting all points at the same height (elevation) on a surface
keywords.example: The oval marked h=2 connects all points on the hill that are exactly 2 units high

keywords.explain: The view seen when looking straight down from above; shows contour lines as closed curves
keywords.example: A perfectly conical hill would show concentric circles in top view

keywords.explain: Closely spaced contours in top view = steep slope; widely spaced = gentle slope
keywords.example: If the hill is steeper near the base, the outer contours are closer together than inner ones

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 180' width='340' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='70' y='22' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Side view</text><ellipse cx='70' cy='80' rx='55' ry='30' fill='none' stroke='#8B6D3F' stroke-width='1.5'/><ellipse cx='70' cy='75' rx='38' ry='22' fill='none' stroke='#8B6D3F' stroke-width='1.5'/><ellipse cx='70' cy='70' rx='22' ry='13' fill='none' stroke='#8B6D3F' stroke-width='1.5'/><ellipse cx='70' cy='66' rx='9' ry='6' fill='none' stroke='#8B6D3F' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='130' y='22' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Top view (Q)</text><ellipse cx='205' cy='90' rx='65' ry='45' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='205' cy='90' rx='50' ry='33' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='205' cy='90' rx='33' ry='22' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><ellipse cx='205' cy='90' rx='16' ry='11' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='205' cy='90' r='4' fill='#D85A30'/><text x='205' y='150' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Concentric ovals, peak at center</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Side view of a hill showing nested ovals at increasing heights, and the corresponding top-view contour diagram Q showing concentric oval contours with the peak at the center.

given.aim: Match the side-profile hill with ovals at different heights to the correct top-view contour map

terms.term: Side view with ovals at heights h
terms.meaning: Each oval in the side view represents a horizontal cross-section of the hill at a given height h
terms.example: At h=0 (ground level) the oval is widest; at the peak it reduces to a point
terms.connects: Each oval in side view corresponds to a closed contour curve in top view

terms.meaning: In top view, each height level gives a closed curve; they appear as nested ovals or circles around the summit
terms.example: Highest contour is smallest (closest to peak); lowest is largest (near base)
terms.connects: The correct top-view option shows concentric ovals — option Q

terms.meaning: If the side view shows symmetric ovals, the top view also shows symmetric (roughly oval) contours centered at the peak
terms.example: A smooth symmetric hill gives smooth concentric ovals in plan view
terms.connects: Eliminates options with asymmetric or incorrectly shaped contours

given.plan: Interpret each oval in the side view as a contour at constant height. In top view, these contours must appear as closed, nested curves (larger at lower heights, smaller at greater heights). Compare each option to find the one where contours are properly nested, symmetric, and decrease in size toward the center (the peak).

steps.title: Understand the side view
steps.apply: The side view shows ovals at increasing heights. The outermost (widest) oval is at the lowest height (near base); successively smaller ovals are at greater heights. The summit is where the smallest oval converges.
steps.note: Think of slicing the hill with horizontal planes; each slice gives one oval

steps.title: Translate to top view
steps.apply: Looking down from above, each horizontal slice (oval) appears as a closed curve. The result is a set of concentric closed curves (contours) with the smallest innermost (highest point) and largest outermost (lowest contour at base).
steps.note: The relative spacing reflects the slope: closely packed = steep, widely spread = gentle

steps.title: Identify the correct option
steps.apply: Option Q shows properly nested, concentric oval contours with correct hierarchy (innermost = summit). Options P, R, S have incorrect contour arrangements (e.g., non-nested, asymmetric, or wrong orientation relative to the given side profile).
steps.note: Option Q correctly represents the hill's smooth, symmetric profile as nested closed contours

solution.result: Option Q correctly depicts the top view of the hill

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula applies; this is a spatial reasoning question about reading topographic contour maps — contours are horizontal cross-sections viewed from above.
